title: Lesson 232
transcript: We've been talking about how we need to protect architectures and we said that security should be involved right from the start and throughout the engineering lifecycle phases, for example. But one of the things that we really have to worry about and think about is that a lot of our security that we depend on today is actually purchased from vendors. We buy a lot of products from vendors that provide security for us. We buy firewalls, we buy intrusion detection systems, we buy data loss prevention capabilities, we buy SIEM solutions for logging and monitoring. what we rely on from a organization's perspective from a security perspective relies on products that we buy from vendors now the problem is how do we trust what the vendors tell us because let's say we're in the market for a firewall for example every vendor out there is going to say that their firewall is the best one and how do we really know that that's true and so it'd be nice to have some sort of measurement system that would provide us objective and independent measurements, for example, of the security capabilities of certain products that we can buy from vendors. And that's what we call evaluation criteria. So if you look at This slide here it's meant to highlight the problem that exists today because every vendor out there is going to say that their product is the best one. Whether that's true or not based on our requirements is something that we need to be able to certify for example. understand fully. And so a measurement system for security products and their capabilities is what we call in the industry evaluation criteria. So these measurement systems do actually exist and they've been around for a very long time and there are three significant systems that exist that can provide these measurements that we need. And the very first one is called the Orange Book. The second one is the European equivalent or improvement of the Orange Book called the ITSEC. And the third one, which we're gonna talk about later, is called the Common Criteria. And this is really a historical evolution of how we've evolved in being able to measure for example security products that we buy from vendors. Before we get into the Orange Book and the European equivalent of it which we call the ITSEC. We need to understand these very important concepts that relate to this and those are certification and accreditation. And what you want to remember about these two concepts is that they're always tied together. Accreditation will always follow certification certification will always be followed by accreditation and here are the perfect definitions that you're probably going to see word from word but certification is the comprehensive technical analysis of a product or a solution to make sure that it meets your needs for example if we have to buy a firewall let's say we need to protect our network from the internet. The obvious solution that we would implement is a firewall, but there could be several firewalls out there that would meet our needs. Which one would be best? Well that would require us to go out there and take a look at the different firewalls that we've decided to look at and to certify that each one of them would work for what our requirements actually are. That's the certification process. We are certifying We're doing a comprehensive technical analysis of the capabilities of each of those firewalls to make sure that it meets our needs, for example. But then let's say that we find that three of them would work. We're not going to implement all three firewalls. we're only going to implement one and it's probably going to be the most cost-effective one but that is what accreditation is accreditation as you see here as definition is going to be the official management decision to actually operate and implement one of those solutions and accreditation is typically done for a set period of time. Management would decide that we're going to use this firewall for the next 18 months at the end of which we're going to have to go through the entire certification and accreditation process again. Now at that time we may find that the same solution that we have been using for the last 18 months is still the best one at which point we would re-accredit that solution for whatever we decide at that point. And by we I don't mean the security function. Accreditation is never done by the security function. It is done by management or the owners. And so that's a key distinction that we need to apply here. Certification security does get involved in. but the accreditation part is always done by management and for a set period of time. Now let's get into these three measurement systems that have been built to allow us to trust products that we buy from vendors. The very first one is called the Orange Book. And you can see why it's called the Orange Book. Its cover is actually orange. And it's part of what we call the Rainbow Series, a series of documents that have been put out by the Department of Defense in the United States. Each one of these documents and these publications have something to do with security. For example, there's a light blue book that deals with password guidelines. There's a red book that deals with network security. but the orange one or the one that has the orange cover is what deals with evaluation criteria or being able to measure the security capabilities of products so that we can trust them. Now one of the things that we have to remember about these products or these systems, evaluation criteria systems, is that they need to be objective and independent. They would not work if we allowed the vendors themselves to measure their own products. And so the way that this works is let's say we are a vendor that wants to sell our product and we've designed it and built it and we're ready to market it and sell it. we would hire a, for example, Orange Book licensed organization to come in and rate our product, and if it gets a good rating, then people out there are gonna buy it, not because we, the vendor, are saying it's a good product, but because the independent and objective measurement system says it's a good product. So these systems are really good, not only from a consumer's perspective, Because we can trust the ratings that their products get because they've been given by independent objective organizations. But it's also good for the vendors themselves because once they rate their products, they can compare them to other products that exist out there that maybe their competitors are putting out. and so they want to probably have the same features or better features that other products have from competitors. Now the Orange Book is something that was developed back in the mid 80s and if you look at what architectures and products looked like back then They look much different than they do today. And so a lot of people say the Orange Book is obsolete, but it really isn't from one perspective. First of all, the Orange Book only measures confidentiality. And there's nothing better out there even today to measure some product that you might be interested in that only addresses confidentiality. I mean, when you think about where the Rainbow series and the Orange Book came from, It came from the Department of Defense. They're obviously an organization that's really focused on confidentiality. So the one thing you need to remember about the Orange Book is that it only measures confidentiality. And the other problem that it has is that You know, if you look at what things looked like back in the mid 80s, networks weren't what they are today. And so this system does not work well with anything that is meant to run in a network. It's really meant for standalone, type of box architectures for example single devices those sorts of things that are not meant for a network. Now when you look at the classifications or the ratings that the Orange Book uses it's what you see here on your screen and we basically start at the bottom And as we go up we get more secure. So a product for example that is A1 capable would be a most secure product. In fact I don't think anything has ever received a rating of A1 because that system would obviously be the ultimate from a security perspective. from a confidentiality perspective. Most firewalls, for example, end up being at the B1 level. Most operating systems that exist out there typically end up being at the C2 level. And what you need to remember here is that as we go up, each of the levels will inherit all of the characteristics of the previous levels. So for example, if we go to B1, We have everything that's included in C2 plus they add what's listed there under B1 which is security labels. So B1 would include everything that's below it plus security labeling and now it's one level that for example we need to remember as far as the exam is concerned. at what level of the Orange Book is labeling first a requirement and the answer is as you can see there B1 and as you keep going up we have more security requirements that need to be met. But basically this is what the Orange Book looks like. Now if you remember the two problems that the Orange Book has. First of all it only measures confidentiality. And secondly, it's only meant for single box type of architectures that don't work well in a network environment. The Europeans came along and they looked at the Orange Book and they said, wow, this is a great idea. but as usual you North Americans don't do a good job of something so we're going to take it and improve it and that's basically what the ITSec is. Think of the ITSec as the orange book with those problems that the Orange Book has addressed. In other words, this not only measures confidentiality, but it can also measure integrity and availability requirements. And this, the ITsec, works well in a network environment. And what you need to remember about the ITsec is that one of the other improvements that they made is that there are two measurements that are given according to the ITsec. One for functional requirements. and one for assurance requirements. Because if you remember back to what we discussed as being good characteristics of any control, any control really needs to apply functionality really well. but also the assurance that it's making the right decisions. And I think the example that illustrates this very well would be a firewall. The functional requirements of a firewall is that it needs to be able to allow or block traffic. And that is the functional pillar, the functional requirement. But what assurance do we have that that firewall is actually making the right decisions on a regular basis is the assurance part of how we implement that firewall. So those two pillars need to be addressed. The other improvement that the Europeans made with this measurement system or evaluation criteria is that they said let's look at products and give them two ratings, one for functionality and one for assurance because you could have products that do a really good job of functionality but maybe not so much assurance or vice versa. And so it'd be nice to have two ratings and that's what this gives. Now what you need to remember is that the ITSec uses the same classification levels for functionality as the Orange Book. So what you just saw on the previous slide for example, the classification levels that apply to the orange book are the ones that the ITSec also uses for functionality. So we've got level D where there isn't much security. And then we get into the C level, C1, C2, and then B1, B2, B3, and A1. All right, so those are the functional ratings that you get according to the ITSEC. But then there's also E level assurance. ratings that they created as part of this and what you see on the next slide here are the E ratings. Now you don't need to memorize these but all you need to know is that we go from E0 which means that that product does not provide very good assurance all the way up to E6 which means that product does give good assurance capability and so as we go up we increase the ability of that product to provide assurance.
